info.title: Global Earthquake History: 1M+ Since 1900
info.subtitle: Every M2.5+ earthquake from 1900 to 2026 — magnitude, depth, coordinates
info.description:
Every significant earthquake (magnitude 2.5+) recorded by the USGS since 1900 — over 1 million events spanning 126 years of seismic history.

Downloaded directly from the USGS Earthquake Catalog API (no API key required). Uses half-year query chunks from 2000 onward to stay under the API's 20K event limit and capture every single event without data loss.

Columns:
- id: Unique USGS event identifier
- time: UTC timestamp
- year, month, day_of_year, hour: Extracted time components
- latitude, longitude: Epicenter coordinates
- depth: Depth in km
- mag: Magnitude
- magType: Magnitude type (ml, mb, mw, etc.)
- place: Human-readable location description
- type: Event type (earthquake, explosion, etc.)
- tsunami: 1 if the event generated a tsunami warning, 0 otherwise
- mag_category: Minor / Light / Moderate / Strong / Major / Great / Massive
- depth_category: Shallow (0-70km) / Intermediate (70-300km) / Deep (300-700km)
- nst: Number of seismic stations used
- gap: Largest azimuthal gap between stations (degrees)
- dmin: Distance to nearest station (degrees)
- rms: Root mean square travel time residual (seconds)
- net: Contributing network ID
- status: reviewed or automatic

Key stats:
- 1,054,000+ earthquakes
- Date range: 1900 to present
- 120+ M8+ events (including the 1960 Chile M9.5 and 2011 Japan M9.1)
- 1,100+ M7+ events
- Global coverage with clear Ring of Fire, Mid-Atlantic Ridge, and Alpide Belt patterns

Use cases:
- Geospatial analysis and tectonic boundary visualization
- Magnitude prediction with machine learning
- Gutenberg-Richter law verification
- Seismic hazard and risk modeling
- Time series analysis of earthquake frequency
- Clustering to discover tectonic plate boundaries (DBSCAN)
- Natural disaster research and education
